does: This survey systematically examines security vulnerabilities in large language models by categorizing threats into two primary domains: prompt hacking (including prompt injection and jailbreaking attacks) and adversarial attacks (including data poisoning and backdoor attacks). For each threat category, the authors describe attack mechanisms, analyze their potential impacts on LLM behavior and outputs, and evaluate corresponding mitigation strategies. The survey maps relationships between different vulnerability types and their associated defenses, synthesizing current knowledge about how these attacks operate and which defensive frameworks prove effective at protecting LLMs from exploitation. The work provides a structured taxonomy that connects specific threat models to concrete protection mechanisms.
connects: This survey builds on foundational work in adversarial machine learning and robustness, extending earlier defenses developed for image classifiers and traditional NLP models to the unique challenges posed by large language models. It engages with emerging research on prompt-based attacks that exploit the instruction-following capabilities central to modern LLMs, responding to real-world incidents where systems like ChatGPT were manipulated through crafted inputs. The work synthesizes insights from data poisoning literature and backdoor attack research while addressing how these classical adversarial concepts manifest differently when applied to language models with billions of parameters. It draws on prior security frameworks from AI safety and integrates recent findings about LLM vulnerabilities discovered through automated and manual red-teaming efforts.
enables: This survey provides a structured foundation for future defensive research by identifying gaps in current protections and highlighting relationships between seemingly disparate attack vectors. The systematic categorization enables researchers to develop more comprehensive defense strategies that address multiple threat types coherently rather than in isolation. The work points toward developing certified robustness guarantees for LLMs, designing input validation and filtering mechanisms that scale to model size, and creating training procedures that make models resistant to both prompt-based and data-driven attacks. It supports the development of better evaluation frameworks and red-teaming methodologies, facilitating collaboration between security researchers and practitioners building production LLM systems, ultimately contributing to more resilient and trustworthy AI deployment at scale.
